title: Cambridge CAE 2 — Test 3 (Reading & Use of English)

sections.title: Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)
sections.instructions: You are going to read three extracts which are all concerned in some way with making decisions. For questions 1–6, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text.
sections.passageHtml:
Extract 1: Decision making in business: Gordon Bethune

Having decided that Continental Airlines was worth saving, the question for Gordon Bethune — brought in to rescue the struggling company in 1994 — was how to do it. He and consultant Greg Brenneman mapped out a turnaround strategy.

In retrospect their plan seems commonsensical and obvious, but at the time the opposite appeared closer to the truth, for two reasons. First, the company was in the midst of a crisis. (At one of its darkest moments, it was less than six weeks away from not having enough cash to meet the payroll.) And the second point is this: straightforward as the strategy might have been, no one had ever come up with, nor implemented, such a plan. (The airline had gone through 10 presidents in 10 years.)

‘The first step was to figure out exactly what we wanted to do,’ Bethune says. ‘We needed to develop a simple clear strategy, one that everyone could understand, and once it was in place, one that we weren't going to deviate from.’

Easier said than done. When you are in the middle of an emergency — and trying to save an airline that is desperately short of cash certainly qualified as an emergency — there is a natural tendency to grasp at straws, to go after whatever will bring in money to keep you solvent for another week or two. Bethune understood the temptation and fought it off.

Extract from a novel: Children’s toys

Left to himself, Eustace fell into a day-dream. He thought of his toys and tried to decide which of them he should give to his sister Barbara; he had been told he must part with some of them, and indeed it would not make much difference if they were hers by right, since she already treated them as such. Whenever he went to take them from her she resisted with loud screams. Eustace realised that she wanted them but he did not think she ought to have them. She could not use them intelligently, and besides, they belonged to him. He might be too old to play with them but they brought back the past in a way that nothing else did. Certain moments in the past were like buried treasure to Eustace, living relics of a golden age which it was an ecstasy to contemplate. His toys put him in touch with these secret jewels of experience; they could not perform the miracle if they belonged to someone else. But on the single occasion when he had asserted his ownership and removed the toy rabbit from Barbara, who was sucking its ears, nearly everyone had been against him and there was a terrible scene.

Extract: Children’s involvement in family decision making

In Britain, as children’s rights to citizenship have strengthened over recent years, a presumption in favour of involving children in decisions on matters that directly affect them has developed in a number of areas of law, public policy and professional practice (for example, school councils). Yet surprisingly little is known about how far children’s participation extends to their home lives and the routine business of everyday life. A recent study, based on group discussions and in-depth interviews with 117 children aged between eight and ten, examined how and to what extent the children were involved in shaping their own and their families’ domestic lives.

Many of the findings were illuminating. The ways in which families made decisions involved a subtle, complex and dynamic set of processes in which children could exert a decisive influence. Most families operated democratically but children accepted the ultimate authority of their parents, provided that they felt their parents acted ‘fairly’. For children, ‘fairness’ had more to do with being treated equitably than simply having the decision made in their favour. Children could use claims to fairness as a moral lever in negotiations with parents. Family precedent, especially that set by older brothers and sisters, was an influential factor in ‘good decision making’ and was a more common point of reference than the experience of peers and other families.

questionGroups.instruction: Choose the correct letter A, B, C or D.

questions.instruction: 1. The writer believes that when Bethune and Brenneman produced their plan,
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 — Test 3, Trang 70 Chọn A — 'it seemed unlikely to achieve its objectives.'. - **Chi tiết trong bài:** Tác giả viết: 'In retrospect their plan seems commonsensical and obvious, but at the time the opposite appeared closer to the truth, for two reasons...' (Nhìn lại thì kế hoạch có vẻ hợp lý và hiển nhiên, nhưng vào thời điểm đó điều ngược lại gần với sự thật hơn...). - **Giải thích:** Cụm 'the opposite appeared closer to the truth' (điều ngược lại có vẻ đúng hơn) ở đây ám chỉ kế hoạch trông không có vẻ gì là hợp lý, hiển nhiên hay khả thi (tức là dường như khó đạt được mục tiêu). - **Lý do loại trừ các phương án khác:** - ❌ **B** (nó là cách tốt nhất để huy động tiền nhanh): Bài viết đề cập Continental đang khủng hoảng tiền mặt cực độ ('less than six weeks away from not having enough cash'), nhưng kế hoạch không phải là cách huy động tiền nhanh mà là một chiến lược xoay chuyển tình thế dài hạn. - ❌ **C** (nó có vẻ tốn kém quá mức để thực hiện): Bài viết không nói về chi phí thực hiện kế hoạch. - ❌ **D** (nó có vẻ quá giống với các chiến lược trước đây): Ngược lại, bài viết nói chưa có ai từng nghĩ ra hay thực hiện kế hoạch như vậy trước đây ('no one had ever come up with, nor implemented, such a plan'). Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: A. it seemed unlikely to achieve its objectives. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. it seemed unlikely to achieve its objectives.
B. it was the best way to raise money quickly.
C. it was likely to cost too much to implement.
D. it seemed too similar to previous strategies.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 2. What does the writer imply about Bethune?
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 — Test 3, Trang 70 Chọn D — 'He took a long-term view of the situation.'. - **Chi tiết trong bài:** Tác giả phân tích: 'there is a natural tendency to grasp at straws, to go after whatever will bring in money to keep you solvent for another week or two. Bethune understood the temptation and fought it off.' (Có xu hướng tự nhiên là bấu víu vào bất cứ thứ gì mang lại tiền để giữ cho công ty hoạt động thêm một hai tuần. Bethune hiểu cám dỗ đó và đã chống lại nó). - **Giải thích:** Việc chống lại các cám dỗ giải quyết khủng hoảng trước mắt (mang tính chất chắp vá ngắn hạn 'keep you solvent for another week or two') để tập trung vào chiến lược nhất quán đã đề ra cho thấy Bethune có một tầm nhìn dài hạn (long-term view). - **Lý do loại trừ các phương án khác:** - ❌ **A** (ông hiểu tầm quan trọng của việc linh hoạt): Bethune tuyên bố chiến lược của mình là không thay đổi khi đã đi vào thực hiện ('once it was in place, one that we weren't going to deviate from'), tức là đề cao sự kiên định hơn là linh hoạt vô lối. - ❌ **B** (ông có kinh nghiệm đáng kể trong việc đối phó với khủng hoảng): Bài viết không đề cập đến việc trước đây ông đã giải quyết nhiều khủng hoảng tương tự hay chưa. - ❌ **C** (ông đối mặt với sự phản đối từ bên trong công ty): Đoạn trích không nói đến sự phản đối từ nội bộ. Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: D. He took a long-term view of the situation. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. He understood the importance of being flexible.
B. He had considerable experience of dealing with crises.
C. He faced opposition from within the company.
D. He took a long-term view of the situation.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 3. In this extract the writer focuses on Eustace’s
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 — Test 3, Trang 71 Chọn B — 'efforts to find a solution to a dilemma.'. - **Chi tiết trong bài:** Eustace rơi vào trạng thái mơ màng ('fell into a day-dream') suy nghĩ về đồ chơi của mình và cố gắng quyết định xem nên cho em gái Barbara món nào ('tried to decide which of them he should give to his sister Barbara'). Cậu rơi vào tình thế tiến thoái lưỡng nan: một mặt cậu phải cho đi một số món theo yêu cầu của người lớn ('he had been told he must part with some of them'), mặt khác cậu không muốn vì chúng gắn liền với những ký ức tuổi thơ quý giá ('they brought back the past in a way that nothing else did'). Việc cố gắng chọn lựa chính là nỗ lực tìm kiếm giải pháp cho tình thế khó xử này. - **Lý do loại trừ các phương án khác:** - ❌ **A** (mong muốn cải thiện mối quan hệ với em gái): Eustace không nghĩ em mình nên có những món đồ chơi đó ('did not think she ought to have them') và em gái luôn la hét khi cậu lấy đồ chơi lại, không có chi tiết thể hiện cậu muốn cải thiện tình cảm. - ❌ **C** (mong muốn gia tăng số lượng tài sản sở hữu): Eustace đang phải cho bớt đồ chơi đi chứ không phải muốn có thêm. - ❌ **D** (cảm giác bản thân khác biệt so với người khác): Eustace cảm thấy cô độc khi bị mọi người phản đối trong vụ giật lại chú thỏ bông, nhưng đây không phải là trọng tâm tập trung xuyên suốt của đoạn văn. Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: B. efforts to find a solution to a dilemma. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. desire to improve his relationship with his sister.
B. efforts to find a solution to a dilemma.
C. wish to increase the number of his possessions.
D. sense of being different from other people.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 4. Eustace liked his toys because of
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 — Test 3, Trang 71 Chọn A — 'incidents associated with them.'. - **Chi tiết trong bài:** Bài viết nêu rõ lý do Eustace yêu quý đồ chơi: 'they brought back the past in a way that nothing else did. Certain moments in the past were like buried treasure to Eustace, living relics of a golden age... secret jewels of experience' (chúng gợi lại quá khứ theo cách không có gì khác làm được. Những khoảnh khắc cụ thể trong quá khứ giống như kho báu bị chôn giấu... những mảnh kỷ vật sống động của một thời hoàng kim...). - **Giải thích:** Những 'moments in the past' (khoảnh khắc trong quá khứ) và 'secret jewels of experience' (những báu vật của trải nghiệm) chính là các sự kiện/sự việc/trải nghiệm cụ thể gắn liền với những món đồ chơi đó. - **Lý do loại trừ các phương án khác:** - ❌ **B** (những người đã tặng đồ chơi cho cậu): Không có thông tin về người tặng. - ❌ **C** (những gì cậu có thể làm với chúng): Cậu tự nhận mình đã quá tuổi chơi chúng ('He might be too old to play with them'). - ❌ **D** (tính chất hiếm và quý giá của chúng): Đồ chơi chỉ có giá trị tinh thần đối với Eustace ('secret jewels of experience') chứ không phải là đồ vật có giá trị vật chất hay quý hiếm thực tế. Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: A. incidents associated with them. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: B. the people who had given them to him.
C. what he could do with them.
D. their rare and valuable nature.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 5. In the first paragraph, the writer implies that
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 — Test 3, Trang 72 Chọn B — 'greater understanding of domestic decision making involving children would be beneficial.'. - **Chi tiết trong bài:** Tác giả nhận xét: 'Yet surprisingly little is known about how far children’s participation extends to their home lives and the routine business of everyday life.' (Tuy nhiên, điều đáng ngạc nhiên là người ta biết rất ít về việc sự tham gia của trẻ em mở rộng đến mức nào trong cuộc sống gia đình và các công việc thường nhật). - **Giải thích:** Việc tác giả dùng từ 'surprisingly little' (ít một cách đáng ngạc nhiên) ngụ ý rằng đây là một khoảng trống tri thức lớn cần được nghiên cứu và việc hiểu rõ hơn về cách trẻ em tham gia vào các quyết định tại gia đình sẽ mang lại nhiều giá trị hữu ích. - **Lý do loại trừ các phương án khác:** - ❌ **A** (sự tham gia của trẻ em đang gây hại cho chất lượng quyết định công cộng): Ngược lại, bài viết nói quyền công dân của trẻ em được tăng cường và có xu hướng khuyến khích trẻ tham gia. - ❌ **C** (trẻ em sẽ đưa ra quyết định tốt hơn nếu chúng hiểu rõ hơn về quy trình): Bài viết chưa thảo luận về việc chất lượng quyết định của trẻ tốt lên hay xấu đi. - ❌ **D** (trẻ em rất muốn tham gia nhiều hơn vào việc ra quyết định trong gia đình): Đoạn trích chỉ nói về việc thực hiện nghiên cứu để tìm hiểu xem chúng tham gia như thế nào chứ chưa khẳng định mức độ mong muốn của trẻ. Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: B. greater understanding of domestic decision making involving children would be beneficial. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. the involvement of children is damaging the quality of public decision making.
B. greater understanding of domestic decision making involving children would be beneficial.
C. children would make better decisions if they understood the decision-making process better.
D. children are keen to become more involved in domestic decision making.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 6. According to the second paragraph, children tended to accept decisions that
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 — Test 3, Trang 72 Chọn B — 'were made in a way that conformed with their sense of justice.'. - **Chi tiết trong bài:** Tác giả viết: 'children accepted the ultimate authority of their parents, provided that they felt their parents acted ‘fairly’. For children, ‘fairness’ had more to do with being treated equitably than simply having the decision made in their favour.' (trẻ em chấp nhận quyền lực tuyệt đối của cha mẹ, với điều kiện chúng cảm thấy cha mẹ hành động 'công bằng'. Đối với trẻ em, 'sự công bằng' liên quan nhiều đến việc được đối xử bình đẳng hơn là việc quyết định đó có lợi cho chúng). - **Giải thích:** Khái niệm 'fairly' (công bằng) và 'treated equitably' (đối xử công bằng/bình đẳng) tương ứng với 'sense of justice' (ý thức về công lý/sự công bằng) của trẻ em. - **Lý do loại trừ các phương án khác:** - ❌ **A** (tương tự như các quyết định của các gia đình khác): Ngược lại, bài viết nói tiền lệ gia đình từ anh chị lớn ('Family precedent') là điểm tham chiếu phổ biến hơn là trải nghiệm của bạn bè hay gia đình khác ('experience of peers and other families'). - ❌ **C** (có lợi cho chúng hơn là anh chị em): Bài viết nhấn mạnh sự công bằng trong đối xử ('treated equitably') chứ không phải là sự thiên vị có lợi hơn cho bản thân. - ❌ **D** (được đưa ra bằng cách trao cho trẻ quyền lực ngang bằng cha mẹ): Trẻ em vẫn chấp nhận quyền lực tối cao của cha mẹ ('accepted the ultimate authority of their parents') miễn là công bằng, chứ không đòi hỏi quyền lực ngang hàng. Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: B. were made in a way that conformed with their sense of justice. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. were similar to decisions made by other families.
B. were made in a way that conformed with their sense of justice.
C. were more favourable to them than to their brothers and sisters.
D. were made by giving the child as much authority as their parents.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

sections.title: Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)
sections.instructions: You are going to read an extract from a newspaper article about working from home. Six paragraphs have been removed from the extract. Choose from the paragraphs A-G the one which fits each gap (7-12). There is one extra paragraph which you do not need to use.
sections.passageHtml:
Plugging in the home

Georgina McGuiness had taken a long career break from journalism and she felt out of touch with the changes brought about by technology. She recounts here how she was able to transform the family home into an efficient workplace.

Last year I turned 37 and realised that time was running out if I wanted to resurrect a career in journalism.

A quick glance at my curriculum vitae showed that I was shamefully stuck in the 1980s, when a piece of carbon wedged in between several sheets of paper in a typewriter was the state of the art. It seemed that only a madman would let me loose on a computer in his newsroom. And why did most of the jobs advertised ask for experience in desktop publishing - which I didn't have?

[ Gap 7 ]

Clearly, there was a gaping hole in what was left of my career and I had to act quickly. Leaving home before the children did would be fraught with obstacles, or so I thought until I entered a competition in a local newspaper. Like a success story you read or hear about that only ever happens to other people, my family and I won a computer package.

[ Gap 8 ]

I had everything I would need for working from home - and I could still manage to take the children to school. They were confident with computers from the start, already well versed in them from school. I was much more hesitant, convinced that all my work would disappear without trace if I pressed the wrong button. I could not have been more wrong.

[ Gap 9 ]

I recently began freelancing for a magazine, contributing about two articles a month, and I have become smug in the knowledge that I have the best of two worlds.

So how has the computer helped me? Since my schooldays I have always worked at a desk that can only be described as a chaotic mess.

[ Gap 10 ]

Spreadsheets help keep a record of income and expenses and the Internet means I can research stories, ask for further information on the bulletin board in the journalism or publishing forums and even discuss the pros and cons of working from home with people from all over the world.

[ Gap 11 ]

However, there is a growing band of people who have recently bought multi-media PCs, not just for the educational, leisure and entertainment facilities. In my street alone there appears to be a cottage industry evolving from the sheer convenience of not having to join the commuter struggle into the city each day. So what characterises modern-day home workers?

[ Gap 12 ]

Taking this into account, I seem to fit in well. And who knows, one day I will be emailing a column to a newspaper in Melbourne or, better still, publishing my own magazine from home. It seems the sky, or should I say cyberspace, is the limit.

Paragraph options (A-G):

A. Consequently, I was always losing scraps of paper containing vital bits of information. The computer has transformed me into an organised worker, particularly when it comes to office administration.

B. If all this sounds too good to be true, there is a dark side to computing from home. You can be in isolation from physical human contact and also there are the distractions of putting urgent jobs about the house first.

C. To get an idea of the speed and convenience with which someone based at home can send their work back to the office, this article will be sent in a matter of minutes via a modem straight into the editor's computer.

D. I thought I had a better chance of hosting a seminar in nuclear physics than attempting to lay out a page on a computer. I was the family technophobe; even pocket calculators were a mystery to me and I still don't know how to use the timer on the DVD.

E. A recent report was unable to give an exact profile. Home-office workers comprise both males and females, aged between 25 and 55. However, they are usually well educated and more likely to be working in sales, marketing or technology.

F. Though far from being adroit, I did manage to learn the basic skills I needed - it was all so logical, easy and idiot-proof. And, like everything that you persevere with, you learn a little more each day.

G. Supplied with a laptop computer to free my husband from his desk, and a personal computer for us all, we dived in at the deep end. The children forsook the television and I set up a mini-office in a corner of the kitchen with my computer linking me to the information superhighway.

questionGroups.instruction: Select the correct paragraph (A–G) for each gap.

questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 - Test 3, Reading Part 2, Trang 62-63. Chọn D. Gap 7 follows the writer admitting she lacks desktop-publishing experience. Option D explains her technophobia in concrete terms, then the next paragraph moves to the “gaping hole” in her career and the need to act quickly. Method: kiểm tra tham chiếu trước/sau gap và hướng lập luận. Trap: option đúng thường nối hai phía, không chỉ lặp keyword “computer” hoặc “home”.
questions.answerLocation: I thought I had a better chance of hosting a seminar in nuclear physics
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)

questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 - Test 3, Reading Part 2, Trang 62-63. Chọn G. Gap 8 comes after winning a computer package. Option G specifies what the package included and how the family set up a mini-office, which leads naturally into “I had everything I would need for working from home.” Method: kiểm tra tham chiếu trước/sau gap và hướng lập luận. Trap: option đúng thường nối hai phía, không chỉ lặp keyword “computer” hoặc “home”.
questions.answerLocation: Supplied with a laptop computer to free my husband from his desk
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)

questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 - Test 3, Reading Part 2, Trang 62-63. Chọn F. Gap 9 follows her fear that one wrong button would erase her work. Option F resolves that fear by saying she learned the basic skills and improved day by day; the next paragraph reports she is now freelancing successfully. Method: kiểm tra tham chiếu trước/sau gap và hướng lập luận. Trap: option đúng thường nối hai phía, không chỉ lặp keyword “computer” hoặc “home”.
questions.answerLocation: I did manage to learn the basic skills I needed
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)

questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 - Test 3, Reading Part 2, Trang 62-63. Chọn A. The previous sentence says her desk was a chaotic mess. Option A starts “Consequently” and explains losing scraps of paper, then says the computer made her organised. The following sentence gives examples: spreadsheets, invoices and letterheads. sections.title: Part 3: Multiple Choice (Reading)
sections.instructions: You are going to read a newspaper article. For questions 13-19, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text.
sections.passageHtml:
Solar Survivor

Charles Clover ventures inside Britain's most environmentally friendly home.

Southwell in Nottinghamshire is full of surprises. The first is Britain's least-known ancient cathedral, Southwell Minster, celebrated by writers of an environmental disposition for the pagan figures of 'green' men which medieval craftsmen carved into the decorations in its thirteenth-century chapter house. The second, appropriately enough, is Britain's greenest dwelling, the 'autonomous house', designed and built by Robert and Brenda Vale.

The Vales use rainwater for washing and drinking, recycle their sewage into garden compost and heat their house with waste heat from electrical appliances and their own body heat, together with that of their three teenage children and their two cats, Edison and Faraday. You could easily miss the traditional-looking house, roofed with clay pantiles, on a verdant corner plot 300 metres from the Minster. It was designed to echo the burnt-orange brick of the town's nineteenth-century buildings and won approval from the planners even though it is in a conservation area.

Ring the solar-powered doorbell and there is total silence. The house is super-insulated, with krypton-filled triple-glazed windows, which means that you do not hear a sound inside. Once inside and with your shoes off (at Robert's insistence), there is a monastic stillness. It is a sunny summer's day, the windows are closed and the conservatory is doing its normal job of warming the air before it ventilates the house. Vale apologises and moves through the house, opening ingenious ventilation shafts and windows. You need to create draughts because draught-proofing is everywhere: even Edison and Faraday have their own air-locked miniature door.

The Vales, who teach architecture at Nottingham University, were serious about the environment long before it hit the political agenda. They wrote a book on green architecture back in the 1970s, The Autonomous House. They began by designing a building which emitted no carbon dioxide. Then they got carried away and decided to do without mains water as well. They designed composting earth closets, lowered rainwater tanks into the cellar, and specified copper gutters to protect the drinking water, which they pass through two filters before use. Water from washing runs into the garden (the Vales don't have a dishwasher because they believe it is morally unacceptable to use strong detergents). Most details have a similar statement in mind.

'We wanted people to see that it was possible to design a house which would be far less detrimental to the environment, without having to live in the dark,' says Robert. 'It would not be medieval.' The house's only medieval aspect is aesthetic: the hall, which includes the hearth and the staircase, rises the full height of the building.

The Vales pay no water bills. And last winter the house used only nine units of electricity a day costing about 70p - which is roughly what other four-bedroomed houses use on top of heating. Soon it will use even less, when ?20,000 worth of solar water heating panels and generating equipment arrive and are erected in the garden. The house will draw electricity from the mains supply for cooking and running the appliances, but will generate a surplus of electricity. There will even be enough, one day, to charge an electric car. The only heating is a small wood-burning stove in the hall, which the Vales claim not to use except in the very coldest weather.

So is it warm in winter? One night in February when I happened to call on him, Robert was sitting reading. It was too warm to light the fire, he said. The room temperature on the first floor was 18°C, less than the generally expected temperature of living areas, but entirely comfortable, he claimed, because there are no draughts, no radiant heat loss, since everything you touch is at the same temperature. Perceived temperature depends on these factors. An Edwardian lady in the early years of the twentieth century was entirely comfortable at 12.5°C, he says, because of the insulation provided by her clothing. Those people who live in pre-1900 housing, he suggests, should simply go back to living as people did then. Somehow, it is difficult to think of this idea catching on.

The house's secret is that it is low-tech and there is little to go wrong. Almost everything was obtained from a builder's merchant and installed by local craftsmen. This made the house cheap to build - it cost the same price per square metre as low-cost housing for rent. Not surprisingly, the commercial building companies are determinedly resisting this idea.

questions.instruction: 13. According to the writer, the exterior of the Vales' house is

Trap: the sentence is about public/political attention, not a recent change in the Vales' own views.

options: A. They have always been a difficult topic.

questions.instruction: 16. What does the writer imply about the decision not to use mains water in the Vales' house?
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 - Test 3, Reading Part 3, Trang 64-65. Chọn C. 'Then they got carried away and decided to do without mains water as well' suggests they went beyond the original environmental design aim. questions.answerLocation: 'Then they got carried away and decided to do without mains water as well.'
options: C. It was an extreme choice.

questions.instruction: 17. In Robert Vale's opinion, his home challenges the idea that houses designed with the environment in mind must be
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 - Test 3, Reading Part 3, Trang 64-65. Chọn B. Robert says the house should show environmental design can work 'without having to live in the dark' and adds 'It would not be medieval.' That rejects the idea of a primitive lifestyle. Trap: 'draughts' appear elsewhere as ventilation, but this opinion is about not being medieval/primitive.
questions.answerLocation: Robert: 'It would not be medieval.'
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 3: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 18. The planned changes to the house's electrical system will mean that
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 - Test 3, Reading Part 3, Trang 64-65. Chọn A. The text says the house will still draw electricity from the mains for some uses, but will 'generate a surplus of electricity'. Trap: B is too absolute because the mains supply is still used for cooking and appliances.
questions.answerLocation: The house will draw from the mains but will 'generate a surplus of electricity'.
options: A. the house will produce more electricity than it uses.
B. the Vales will not use electricity from the mains supply.
C. the house will use more electricity than it does now.
D. the Vales' electricity bills will remain at their current level.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 3: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 19. According to Robert Vale, the house was comfortable in February because
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 - Test 3, Reading Part 3, Trang 64-65. Chọn A. Robert explains that there are no draughts or radiant heat loss because everything you touch is at the same temperature, so the perceived temperature feels comfortable. Trap: B misreads 18°C: the point is not the number itself, but the absence of temperature variation.
questions.answerLocation: There are no draughts or radiant heat loss, since everything you touch is at the same temperature.
options: A. no variations in temperature could be noticed.
B. 18°C was acceptable for ordinary houses.
C. it was not a particularly cold winter.
D. he had got used to the temperature.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 3: Multiple Choice (Reading)

sections.title: Part 4: Multiple Matching (Reading)
sections.instructions: You are going to read an article about book reviews. For questions 20–34, choose from the publishers (A–E). The publishers may be chosen more than once.
sections.passageHtml:
Do reviews sell books?

Five publishers give their views on the impact of book reviews.

Publisher A: Jeremy Taylor

I tend to ignore reviews that are just short summaries of the plot. What I'm looking for is a critical analysis that helps me understand where the book fits into the wider literary landscape. However, I do think that reviews of books by first-time authors are incredibly important. Without them, it's very difficult to get any attention for a new writer. For established authors, a bad review won't necessarily hurt sales, but for a new author, a good review can make all the difference. I also think that reviews in certain highbrow newspapers are much more influential than those in the popular press.

Publisher B: Sarah Jenkins

It's interesting how reviews of genre fiction, like crime novels or sci-fi, always seem to sound the same. They tend to use the same cliches and formulas. I prefer reviews that go into detail about the story, because that's what my readers care about. I want to know if the plot is gripping and the characters are believable. I also find reviews that compare a new book to other books in the same genre very useful. It helps readers know what to expect. In terms of selling books, I think a review's main value is simply raising awareness that the book exists.

Publisher C: David Miller

In my experience, reviews have very little direct impact on book sales. A glowing review in a national newspaper might result in a temporary blip in sales, but it's rarely sustained. What really sells books is word of mouth and author promotion. I prefer short, punchy reviews that get straight to the point. Long, academic essays in literary journals might be intellectually stimulating, but they don't do much to persuade people to buy the book. The most effective reviews are those published in the weekend broadsheets; they seem to have a much higher conversion rate than reviews published during the week.

Publisher D: Emma Watson

I'm always surprised by the reviews of the books we publish. Sometimes a book we think is brilliant gets ignored or panned, while a book we had low expectations for is hailed as a masterpiece. But regardless of what the reviewers say, I don't think it affects the bottom line very much. A positive review is nice for the author's ego, but it doesn't guarantee sales. We've had books that received rave reviews but still failed to sell. On the other hand, we've had books that were completely ignored by the critics but became bestsellers. Word of mouth is far more powerful.

Publisher E: Robert Davies

A really good review can sometimes backfire. If a reviewer builds a book up too much, readers can end up disappointed when they actually read it. It's much better to have moderate, balanced reviews that set realistic expectations. I value reviews that compare different books because it gives a sense of perspective. It's helpful to know if an author's new book is better or worse than their previous one. Ultimately, I think reviews are just one part of the marketing mix. They are important for building an author's reputation over the long term, but they aren't the silver bullet for sales.

sections.passageHtml:
Holidays in South Carolina

South Carolina is a state of great contrast. From the quiet beauty of the Blue Ridge Mountains to the bustling activity of the Grand Strand, there is something for everyone. If you are looking for a relaxing beach holiday, the Grand Strand is the (0) [ B ] place. With over 60 miles of continuous sandy beach, it is one of the most popular holiday destinations in the country.

But there is much more to South Carolina than just beaches. The state is (1) ______ of history and culture. Charleston, for example, is famous for its historic buildings and cobblestone streets. Visitors can take a carriage tour of the city or visit one of the many plantations in the (2) ______ area. For those interested in outdoor activities, the state offers plenty of opportunities for hiking, fishing and boating. The Blue Ridge Mountains, in the northwest (3) ______ of the state, are particularly popular with hikers and nature lovers.

South Carolina is also home to a (4) ______ of unique festivals and events. The Spoleto Festival USA, held annually in Charleston, is one of the world's premier performing arts festivals. Other popular events include the South Carolina State Fair in Columbia and the Chitlin' Strut in Salley. No matter what your interests, you are sure to find something to (5) ______ you in South Carolina. The state's mild climate and friendly people make it a welcoming destination at any time of (6) ______.

In (7) ______ to its natural beauty and cultural attractions, South Carolina is also known for its delicious cuisine. Southern cooking, with its emphasis on fresh seafood and local ingredients, is a highlight of any visit. From traditional shrimp and grits to modern interpretations of Southern classics, there is a wide (8) ______ of dining options to choose from.

Whether you are planning a family holiday, a romantic getaway or a solo adventure, South Carolina is sure to (9) ______ your expectations. With so much to see and do, it's no (10) ______ that so many visitors return year after year. So why not start planning your South Carolina holiday today? You won't be disappointed by the (11) ______ of experiences this beautiful state has to (12) ______.

questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 — Test 3, Trang 81 Chọn B — 'right'. Cụm từ 'right in the middle of' hoặc cấu trúc tương ứng. Ở đây, từ phù hợp nhất về nghĩa và kết hợp từ (collocation) để mô tả vị trí hoặc tính chất lịch sử là 'right' (chính xác, thực sự) khi đi kèm ngữ cảnh miêu tả lịch sử. - ❌ Các từ 'direct', 'precise', 'exact' không tạo thành kết hợp từ tự nhiên phù hợp trong văn cảnh này. Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Ở đây danh từ phía sau là 'history and culture' là các danh từ trừu tượng không đếm được. - ❌ **B** 'amount' và **C** 'number' không kết hợp đúng ngữ pháp ở đây (number đi với danh từ đếm được số nhiều; amount thường đi kèm giới từ khác hoặc dùng trong 'a large amount of' chứ không tự nhiên bằng 'a great deal of' trong văn cảnh này). - ❌ **D** 'size' sai nghĩa hoàn toàn. sections.passageHtml:
The Sahara marathon

The Marathon of the Sands is a six-day ultramarathon, widely regarded as the toughest footrace (0) [ ON ] Earth. It is held annually in the Moroccan Sahara desert, (13) ______ competitors must cover a total distance of 150 miles in temperatures that can reach 50 degrees Celsius.

For the runners, (14) ______ is not just a test of endurance, but also of self-sufficiency. They must carry all their own food, sleeping gear and medical supplies in a backpack. The organisers provide water, but (15) ______ allocation is strictly limited. Anyone who exceeds (16) ______ limit is penalised.

The race attracts competitors from all over the world, (17) ______ range from elite athletes to adventurous amateurs. Many of them train for months, or even years, (18) ______ preparation for the event. However, (19) ______ the intense physical preparation, nothing can truly prepare you for the harsh reality of the desert. Sand dunes, rocky plains and blistering heat make (20) ______ step a struggle. In (21) ______, the weight of the backpack takes a heavy toll (22) ______ the body, leading to blisters, exhaustion and dehydration.

Yet, despite the hardships, the race has a high completion rate. Most runners are driven by a deep sense of personal challenge or the desire to raise money for charity. For (23) ______ who cross the finish line, the feeling of achievement is immense. It is an experience that stays (24) ______ them for the rest of their lives, and many find (25) ______ signing up for the race again in the future.

Example (0):

0. ON (e.g. 'on Earth')

sections.passageHtml:
Thor Heyerdahl and the Kon-Tiki expedition

Thor Heyerdahl is one of the most famous explorers of the twentieth century. He is (0) [ ASSOCIATED ] in the public mind with the Kon-Tiki expedition, which he led in 1947. Heyerdahl believed that the Polynesian islands had been settled by people from South America, rather than Asia, in pre-Columbian times. To prove this was possible, he built a raft out of balsa wood, named it Kon-Tiki, and sailed it 4,300 miles from Peru to Tuamotu.

The expedition was a major success, and the book Heyerdahl wrote about it became an international bestseller. In the public mind, Heyerdahl's name is (28) ______ linked with Kon-Tiki. However, his theories were not (29) ______ accepted by the scientific community. Many anthropologists pointed to the many (30) ______ between Polynesian and Asian languages and cultures as proof that the islands had been settled from the west.

Despite the criticism, Heyerdahl remained convinced that he was right. He argued that the oceans were not obstacles to ancient peoples, but rather highways that facilitated (31) ______ communication. To demonstrate the (32) ______ of ancient vessels, he built several other replica ships, including Ra and Tigris, and sailed them across the Atlantic and Indian Oceans. Heyerdahl's voyages showed that ancient peoples had the technology to cross vast (33) ______ of water. Even though his theories are now largely discounted, his courage and determination are still admired. He proved that with enough faith and effort, the seemingly (34) ______ can be achieved. His life is a testament to the power of human curiosity and the desire to explore the unknown, despite the (35) ______ risks. The (36) ______ of his raft with the reef at Raroia was a dramatic end to a voyage that will always be remembered as a classic of modern exploration. It provided (37) ______ of what can be achieved against all odds.

Example (0):

0. ASSOCIATED (from 'ASSOCIATE')

Q49. The panel began the programme by discussing the media.
